seoTitle: How to Track Changes in Google Docs (2026 Guide)
metaDescription: Use Suggesting mode in Google Docs to track edits, review color-coded suggestions, and accept or reject changes from collaborators.
intro: Collaborating on a shared document without a proper edit-tracking workflow can lead to confusing overwrites. This guide covers switching to Suggesting mode, reviewing color-coded edits, and using Version History to audit the full change log.

steps.heading: Switch to Suggesting mode
steps.body: Click the pencil icon in the top right of your document and choose Suggesting from the dropdown to start tracking edits.

steps.heading: Make your suggested edits
steps.body: Type new text or delete content, and Google Docs will color-code your changes and open an inline suggestion box.

steps.heading: Accept or reject suggestions
steps.body: Click the checkmark in the margin to accept an edit, or the X to reject it and revert the text.

steps.heading: Comment on a suggestion
steps.body: Reply inside an active suggestion box to discuss a specific wording choice with your collaborators.

steps.heading: Review the full change history
steps.body: Click File, hover over Version History, and select 'See Version History' to audit every past edit and contributor.

faq.question: How do I know if I'm in Suggesting mode?
faq.answer: The pencil icon in the top right will display 'Suggesting' rather than 'Editing' when the mode is active.

faq.question: Can I force collaborators into Suggesting mode only?
faq.answer: Yes, adjust the document's sharing permissions to give external users 'Commenter' access, which limits them to suggestions.

faq.question: What happens if I accept all suggestions at once?
faq.answer: There's an option in the suggestion menu to accept or reject all changes in bulk.

faq.question: Do suggestions expire if left unreviewed?
faq.answer: No, suggestions remain pending indefinitely until someone accepts or rejects them.
